notes.anchor: the fifteenth day before the Kalends of January
notes.note: Roman dates were counted inclusively backward from three fixed points: the Kalends (1st), Nones (5th or 7th), and Ides (13th or 15th). The fifteenth day before the Kalends of January is 18 December.

notes.anchor: so as not to come as a burden to the household
notes.note: The Compitalia, a moveable festival of the crossroads-Lares (Lares Compitales) celebrated in early January, fell here on 2 January; Cicero avoids descending on the Alban household on the feast-day itself, when the slaves kept holiday.

notes.anchor: on the strength of the imperium I hold
notes.note: Imperium was the formal power of military command and jurisdiction. Cicero still held his proconsular imperium from Cilicia (not yet laid down, since he hoped for a triumph), which Pompey's circle wanted to redeploy by sending him to govern Sicily.

notes.anchor: for Curio could never have held out
notes.note: C. Scribonius Curio, tribune of 50 BC and now Caesar's agent, used his veto through the year to block any decree assigning successors to Caesar's provinces, keeping them governorless; Cicero blames the Senate for never moving against him.

notes.anchor: the loss of the Campanian land
notes.note: The fertile public land of Campania (the ager Campanus), distributed to Pompey's veterans and the urban poor under Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC; Cicero lists it among the chain of concessions that followed from indulging Caesar.

notes.anchor: the adoption of a patrician by a plebeian
notes.note: The transfer of the patrician P. Clodius into a plebeian family in 59 BC (so he could hold the tribunate), which Cicero blamed for his own exile; he links it here to Caesar's consulship as part of one lawless sequence.

The military reckoning of Caesar's strength after the Gallic wars; Cicero contrasts the ease of resisting Caesar when he was weak with the futility of doing so now that he commands eleven legions, abundant cavalry, and his northern Italian clients.
